metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify which `deps` relation types count as 'linked' for graph exclusion (e.g., 'all CANONICAL_RELATIONS entries: `blocks`, `depends_on`, `relates_to`, `duplicates`, `supersedes`, `discovered_from`') so the implementer doesn't have to guess, and update `test_graph_exclusion` to cover at least `relates_to` in addition to `blocks`/`depends_on`.
findings.finding: The plan instructs `related_ticket_ids` to exclude 'linked tickets in BOTH directions (scan all tickets' deps for any edge whose source OR target is `ticket_id`)' but does not specify which relation types constitute 'linked'. The codebase has six canonical relations: `blocks`, `depends_on`, `relates_to`, `duplicates`, `supersedes`, `discovered_from`. Excluding only `blocks`/`depends_on` would miss `relates_to` (which the system already writes as a reciprocal LINK event); excluding all six may be too broad (e.g., `discovered_from` is a provenance edge, not a semantic relation). The implementer has no guidance on which relation types to include.
findings.location: What section — graph.py description: 'linked tickets in BOTH directions (scan all tickets' deps for any edge whose source OR target is `ticket_id`)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the graph.py description: 'linked = any dep entry whose relation is in CANONICAL_RELATIONS (all six: blocks, depends_on, relates_to, duplicates, supersedes, discovered_from) and whose source or target is ticket_id — this conservatively excludes all semantically-connected tickets.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify whether `retrieve()` calls `related_ticket_ids` internally (and update the Scope interface to include `ticket_id` and `tracker` parameters) OR the caller pre-computes the exclude set (and update the What section and AC2 to say 'graph exclusion is performed by the caller before invoking retrieve()').
findings.finding: The 'What' section describes graph exclusion as happening BEFORE scoring internally ('Graph exclusion BEFORE scoring: new helper graph.py::related_ticket_ids'), strongly implying `retrieve()` calls `related_ticket_ids` itself. But the Scope section's declared interface is `retrieve(query_digest, corpus, exclude:set[str])`, where the exclude set is passed in externally by the caller. AC2 reinforces the internal-call reading by saying 'Candidates EXCLUDE … via `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id, tracker)`' without mentioning that the caller must pre-compute this. An implementer reading these sections faces two directions: wire `related_ticket_ids` inside `retrieve` (What/AC2 reading) vs. accept a pre-computed set from the caller (Scope interface reading). If the intent is that the caller (S7) calls `related_ticket_ids` and passes the result as `exclude`, the What section and AC2 should say so explicitly; if `retrieve` calls it internally, the interface needs `ticket_id` and `tracker` parameters.
findings.location: What section ('Graph exclusion BEFORE scoring'), Scope section (interface: `retrieve(query_digest, corpus, exclude:set[str])`), AC2
findings.suggested_fix: If the intent is that the caller (S7) pre-computes exclusions: update the What section to read 'Graph exclusion: `related_ticket_ids(ticket_id, tracker)` is called by the caller (S7) to build the `exclude` set before calling `retrieve()`'; update AC2 to say 'the caller passes `exclude=related_ticket_ids(ticket_id, tracker)` to `retrieve()`'. If the intent is internal: add `ticket_id: str` and `tracker` to the Scope interface signature.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the `_load_all_states`-double-call contradiction: either (a) have `related_ticket_ids` do its own BFS from `all_states` for descendants (consistent with 'load once') instead of delegating to `list_descendants`, or (b) explicitly drop the 'loads states ONCE' claim and document that two loads occur; pick ONE approach in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan claims `related_ticket_ids` will 'load states ONCE via `_load_all_states(tracker)`', but simultaneously instructs it to call `descendants.list_descendants(ticket_id, tracker)` to derive descendants — and `list_descendants` itself unconditionally calls `_load_all_states(tracker)` internally (descendants.py:40). This contradicts the single-load claim and doubles the I/O cost. At n<1000 this doesn't break correctness, but the stated design rationale ('one state load is sub-ms') is undermined, and the plan's own interface description is internally inconsistent.
findings.location: What section — graph.py description: 'loads states ONCE via `_load_all_states(tracker)` … by calling `descendants.list_descendants(ticket_id, tracker)` and flattening its type-bucketed id lists'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'by calling `descendants.list_descendants(ticket_id, tracker)` and flattening its type-bucketed id lists' with: 'by BFS on the same `all_states` dict (using `children_of` built from `parent_id` links), mirroring `list_descendants`'s internal logic but without a second `_load_all_states` call — keeping the single-load guarantee.'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that graph-read/scoring exceptions are logged at ERROR level with a structured `error=` field, and document (or add) a counter/metric increment so operators can alert on a sustained non-zero error rate rather than relying solely on log grep.
findings.finding: When `retrieve()` silently catches all exceptions and returns `[]`, callers cannot distinguish a genuine 'no candidates found' result from a total graph-read or scoring failure. The plan only specifies 'logged', with no log level, no structured field, and no metric — so a persistent degradation (e.g., state directory unavailable across all tickets in a batch) produces only log noise with no operator-visible signal distinguishable from normal empty results.
findings.location: What section — Errors bullet; Testing — test_error_empty
findings.suggested_fix: In the Errors bullet, specify: 'log at ERROR level with structured fields (ticket_id=, error=); increment a `overlap_retrieve_errors_total` counter (or equivalent observability hook) so persistent failures are distinguishable from genuine empty results.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `_llm_float` (arg-position 2, prefix `""`) to `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS` in `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` so `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_MAX_DOC_FREQ` and `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_MIN_SHOULD_MATCH` appear in `docs/env-vars.md` and the CI drift gate stays green.
findings.finding: The plan adds a new `_llm_float` env-resolution helper to `config.py` but does not mention registering it in `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS` in `scripts/gen_env_registry.py`. The existing scanner at line 116 of `gen_env_registry.py` only recognises helper names in that dict; calls through an unregistered `_llm_float` are silently skipped. As a result, `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_MAX_DOC_FREQ` and `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_MIN_SHOULD_MATCH` (the two float-typed env vars read via the new helper) will be absent from `docs/env-vars.md`, causing the CI env-var drift gate (`.github/workflows/test.yml` runs `python scripts/gen_env_registry.py --check`) to diverge or silently under-report the new surface.
findings.suggested_fix: In `scripts/gen_env_registry.py`, add `"_llm_float": (2, ""),  # llm/config.py: (table, cli, env_name, ...)` to the `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS` dict alongside the existing `_llm_int` entry. Then regenerate `docs/env-vars.md`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify how `graph.py` will access `_load_all_states`: either declare it will import the private function from `descendants.py` (and accept the convention crossing), rename/re-export it as a public helper (e.g. `load_all_states` in a shared `_engine_support` util), or specify that `graph.py` calls `reduce_all_tickets` directly (updating the latency claim accordingly).
findings.finding: `_load_all_states` is a module-private function (underscore-prefixed, no `__all__`, not re-exported) in `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py`. The plan asserts `graph.py` will call it as 'the same helper `descendants.py` uses', but reading the file confirms it is private. While Python does not enforce the convention, importing a private function from a sibling support module is a convention violation that may be flagged in code review, and the plan gives no import-path resolution (no statement that it will be made public, re-exported, or that crossing the privacy boundary is intentional). The committed `graph.py` implementation depends on this working as stated.
findings.location: What section — 'new helper `src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py::related_ticket_ids`… loads states ONCE via `_load_all_states(tracker)` (the same helper `descendants.py` uses)'
findings.suggested_fix: Extract `_load_all_states` into a thin shared module (e.g. `src/rebar/_engine_support/_state_loader.py`) and import it in both `descendants.py` and `graph.py`, making the dependency explicit and convention-clean.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py` to the Scope file-creation list so the `overlap` directory is a proper Python package.
findings.finding: The plan's Scope section enumerates `src/rebar/llm/overlap/retrieve.py` and `src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py` as new files, but omits creating `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py`. The `overlap/` directory does not currently exist, so without an `__init__.py` Python will not treat it as a package and imports from `rebar.llm.overlap.retrieve` or `rebar.llm.overlap.graph` (which S7 will need) will fail at import time.
findings.suggested_fix: Add `src/rebar/llm/overlap/__init__.py` (empty or with a one-line module docstring) to the Scope section alongside `retrieve.py` and `graph.py`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either promote `_load_all_states` to a public API in `descendants.py` (rename to `load_all_states` and enumerate it as a new export), or have `graph.py` use only the public `list_descendants()` API and reconstruct ancestors/siblings from a second BFS, or document the private-import dependency explicitly in the plan.
findings.finding: The plan directs `graph.py` to call `_load_all_states(tracker)` directly from `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py` — a `_`-prefixed (private/internal) symbol — imported across the package boundary from `rebar.llm.overlap` into `rebar._engine_support`. This private import is not enumerated as a consumer impact, and the symbol's private status means it can be refactored without notice.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Confirm `rank-bm25`'s lack of per-field BM25F support with a version-pinned citation (e.g., rank-bm25 PyPI README or source), and add a one-line note to the plan acknowledging `whoosh`'s BM25F support was considered and rejected (e.g., maintenance-only status, heavier dependency footprint) so the bespoke choice has a complete prior-art record.
findings.finding: The plan's central build-vs-reuse justification asserts 'rank-bm25 provides only UNWEIGHTED BM25' as the reason for bespoke implementation, but does not cite a source or acknowledge whoosh, which natively supports BM25F with per-field weights and is available on PyPI. If rank-bm25 actually supports field weighting (e.g., via a post-plan release or an undocumented API), or if whoosh was a viable lighter-weight alternative, the bespoke choice loses its primary stated rationale and the dependency justification weakens.
findings.location: What section: 'rank-bm25 provides only UNWEIGHTED BM25, but we need per-field weighting'
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'What' section, append: 'whoosh provides BM25F with field weights but is in maintenance-only mode (last release 2.7.3, 2013) and adds a non-trivial dependency for a 40 LOC equivalent; rank-bm25 0.8.x (confirmed) implements BM25Okapi/BM25L/BM25Plus only — no field-weight API.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a step in the Scope or Config section instructing that `_llm_float` must be registered in `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS` in `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` (at argument position 2, mirroring the `_llm_int` entry) so the CI env-var drift gate can resolve `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_MAX_DOC_FREQ` and `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_MIN_SHOULD_MATCH`.
findings.finding: The plan adds a new `_llm_float` helper to `config.py` to resolve the two float env vars (`REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_MAX_DOC_FREQ`, `REBAR_LLM_OVERLAP_MIN_SHOULD_MATCH`) but never instructs adding `_llm_float` to `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS` in `scripts/gen_env_registry.py`. The CI drift gate (confirmed in code) AST-scans helper call sites using this registry — any helper not listed is invisible to the scanner. Those two env vars will be absent from `docs/env-vars.md`, causing the CI drift gate to fail or silently omit them.
findings.location: Config section — '`_llm_float` does NOT — add it in `config.py` mirroring `_llm_int`'; `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS` dict (lines 37–46)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to `scripts/gen_env_registry.py` `KNOWN_ENV_HELPERS`: `"_llm_float": (2, ""),  # llm/config.py: (table, cli, env_name, ...)` — this mirrors the existing `_llm_int` entry exactly.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either instruct promoting `_load_all_states` to a semi-public API (rename to `load_all_states` or add to `__all__` in `descendants.py`) or justify the private import explicitly and note it as a known coupling in the Scope section.
findings.finding: `graph.py` is directed to call `_load_all_states(tracker)` imported from `src/rebar/_engine_support/descendants.py`, but `_load_all_states` is a module-private function (underscore-prefixed). The plan does not instruct exporting it (e.g., via `__all__`) or acknowledge the privacy barrier. Importing a private symbol from a sibling module without explicit re-export creates a fragile cross-module coupling that is liable to break if `descendants.py` is refactored.
findings.location: What section — 'new helper `src/rebar/llm/overlap/graph.py::related_ticket_ids` … loads states ONCE via `_load_all_states(tracker)` (the same helper `descendants.py` uses)'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Scope section: 'Rename `_load_all_states` → `load_all_states` in `descendants.py` (remove leading underscore) or add `__all__ = ["list_descendants", "load_all_states"]` to stabilize the cross-module import in `graph.py`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the concrete Python form of `OverlapCandidate` in the Scope section (e.g., `@dataclass` with typed fields `ticket_id: str`, `score: float`, `matched_terms: list[str]`) so S7 can safely depend on attribute access.
findings.finding: The `OverlapCandidate` return type's concrete Python form (dataclass, TypedDict, or NamedTuple) is unspecified; S7 consumes this type and a wrong choice (e.g. TypedDict vs dataclass) could break downstream type-checking or attribute access without the plan clarifying the expectation.
findings.location: What section: 'Returns a typed `OverlapCandidate` `[{ticket_id, score, matched_terms}]`' and Scope: 'Interface for S7'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Scope: '`OverlapCandidate` is a `@dataclass(frozen=True)` with fields `ticket_id: str`, `score: float`, `matched_terms: list[str]`.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the `test_perf` timing methodology in the Testing section (e.g., 'median of 5 back-to-back `retrieve()` calls on a pre-built 300-digest corpus, measured via `time.perf_counter`, excluding corpus construction time').
findings.finding: `test_perf` proves '<10 ms at ~300 digests' but the plan does not specify the timing methodology (wall-clock via `time.perf_counter`, averaged over N runs, single-shot, etc.), leaving the test potentially non-deterministic or trivially gameable on a cold import.
findings.location: Testing section: '`test_perf` (<10 ms at 300 digests)' and AC: 'Returns top-K...< 10 ms at ~300 digests; `pytest ...::{test_topk,test_perf}`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Testing: '`test_perf`: pre-build a 300-digest corpus once, then call `retrieve()` 5 times and assert the median wall-clock time (via `time.perf_counter`) is <10 ms; corpus-construction time is excluded from the budget.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the assertion strategy for `test_field_weights_constant` (e.g., 'mutate `_FIELD_WEIGHTS` for one field to 0.0, call `retrieve()`, and assert that field's terms no longer influence ranking — proving the constant is actually read during scoring').
findings.finding: `test_field_weights_constant` is described only as 'retrieve uses the `_FIELD_WEIGHTS` constant' with no specified assertion mechanism; a trivial implementation could assert `hasattr(retrieve, '_FIELD_WEIGHTS')` without verifying that the constant is actually applied during scoring, making the AC vacuously satisfied.
findings.location: Testing section: '`test_field_weights_constant` (retrieve uses the `_FIELD_WEIGHTS` constant)' and AC5
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Testing: '`test_field_weights_constant`: temporarily patch `retrieve._FIELD_WEIGHTS` so one field has weight 0.0; assert that terms exclusively from that field produce a lower score than when the weight is non-zero, proving the constant governs scoring.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR or CLAUDE.md entry documenting the brute-force BM25F choice over ANN/vector-DB (with the spike results as evidence), the _FIELD_WEIGHTS-as-constant rationale, and the OverlapCandidate interface contract consumed by S7.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a new pipeline stage (Stage-1 BM25F candidate generation) with cross-component coupling to descendants.py internals and a new graph.py module, but does not call for an ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry capturing the architectural decision (brute-force BM25F chosen over ANN/vector-DB, graph-exclusion contract, OverlapCandidate interface).
findings.suggested_fix: Create docs/adr/0XXX-stage1-bm25f-candidate-generation.md (or append to CLAUDE.md under the overlap/ section) with: (1) context — n<1000, latency budget; (2) decision — brute-force BM25F, no ANN; (3) evidence — recall@20=1.0, 0.69 ms @ 800 digests; (4) consequences — field weights are constants, revisit if corpus exceeds ~1000; (5) interface — retrieve() signature for S7.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Rewrite `test_field_weights_constant` as a behavioral test: construct two corpora that differ only in which field contains the matching term (e.g., term in `problem_keywords` vs. `propositions`), run `retrieve()` with the actual `_FIELD_WEIGHTS` values, and assert the higher-weighted field yields a higher score — proving weights influence ranking, not merely that the constant is referenced.
findings.finding: test_field_weights_constant is described as verifying 'retrieve uses the _FIELD_WEIGHTS constant', which reads as a structural/source-grep assertion (the constant is referenced in source) rather than a behavioral test (weighting affects scores). If implemented as a grep, it is a change-detector anti-pattern that locks in a structural detail without proving correctness and could pass even if the weights are misapplied.
findings.location: Testing section / AC line: `test_field_weights_constant (retrieve uses the _FIELD_WEIGHTS constant)`
findings.suggested_fix: In test_field_weights_constant: build digest_A with the query term in problem_keywords (weight 3.0) and digest_B with the same term only in propositions (weight 1.0); assert retrieve() ranks digest_A above digest_B. This proves _FIELD_WEIGHTS is applied, not merely referenced.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify `OverlapCandidate`'s Python container form (dataclass, TypedDict, or NamedTuple) explicitly in the plan, with field types (`ticket_id: str`, `score: float`, `matched_terms: list[str]`), so S7 has an unambiguous access contract.
findings.finding: `OverlapCandidate` is described only as `[{ticket_id, score, matched_terms}]` — a field listing — but the plan never specifies its Python container type (dataclass, TypedDict, or NamedTuple). S7 explicitly consumes `list[OverlapCandidate]`; a mismatch between how `retrieve.py` constructs it and how S7 destructures it (e.g., attribute access vs key access) is a silent interface contract gap.
findings.location: What section — 'Returns a typed `OverlapCandidate` `[{ticket_id, score, matched_terms}]`'; Scope section — 'Interface for S7: `retrieve(query_digest, corpus, exclude:set[str]) -> list[OverlapCandidate]`'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: '`OverlapCandidate` is a `@dataclass(frozen=True)` with fields `ticket_id: str`, `score: float`, `matched_terms: list[str]`, defined in `src/rebar/llm/overlap/retrieve.py` and importable by S7.'

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
